title_en: The Author and His Era: Al-Zamakhshari in the Age of Islamic Learning
title_ar: The Author and His Era: Al-Zamakhshari in the Age of Islamic Learning
content_en:
Abu al-Qasim Mahmud ibn Umar al-Zamakhshari (467–538 AH / 1075–1144 CE) was born in the town of Zamakhshar in the Khwarazm region of Central Asia, in what is today Turkmenistan. He came of age in one of the most intellectually fertile centuries of Islamic civilization, when the great cities of Khurasan, Baghdad, and Nishapur were producing scholars of the highest caliber across every discipline — theology, philosophy, jurisprudence, hadith, grammar, and poetry. Al-Zamakhshari mastered several of these disciplines but achieved his most enduring fame in two: Arabic linguistics and Quranic exegesis.

He was deeply devoted to Arabic as a language and to the Quran as its supreme literary and divine expression. He made multiple journeys to Mecca, the spiritual center of the Islamic world, where he resided for extended periods and earned the honorific title 'Jar Allah' — Neighbor of God — reflecting both his physical proximity to the Haram and the reverence in which the scholarly community held him. He reportedly walked despite a physical disability that caused him considerable hardship, and his dedication to scholarship in the face of this limitation became part of his legendary status.

Al-Zamakhshari was a prolific author. His works span Arabic grammar (the Mufassal, a comprehensive treatment of Arabic morphology and syntax that became a major reference text), Arabic lexicography (al-Fa'iq fi Gharib al-Hadith, on rare vocabulary in hadith literature), adab and proverbs, and theology. But it is his Quranic commentary, Al-Kashaf, that earned him a permanent place in the history of Islamic scholarship — praised by admirers for its linguistic genius and studied critically by scholars who disagree with its theological premises.

His intellectual formation took place within the Mu'tazilite theological school — the rationalist school that flourished particularly in Basra and Baghdad from the second through fifth centuries of the Islamic era, known for its doctrines on the createdness of the Quran, divine justice, and the essential role of reason in theology. By al-Zamakhshari's time, Mu'tazilism had largely been displaced by Ash'ari and Maturidi theology as the mainstream of Sunni orthodoxy, but it continued as a living intellectual tradition in parts of Central Asia and among scholars with rationalist orientations.

Al-Zamakhshari's Mu'tazili commitments are inseparable from his tafsir. The way he reads certain passages, the theological glosses he inserts, and the alternatives he rejects are all shaped by Mu'tazili doctrine. This is why Al-Kashaf is simultaneously one of the most prized and most carefully read texts in the tafsir tradition: its linguistic insights are unparalleled, but its theological interventions must be recognized and evaluated against the Sunni scholarly consensus.
title_id: Pengarang dan Zamannya: Al-Zamakhshari di Era Keilmuan Islam
content_id:
Abu al-Qasim Mahmud ibn Umar al-Zamakhshari (467–538 H / 1075–1144 M) lahir di kota Zamakhshar di wilayah Khwarazm, Asia Tengah, yang kini merupakan bagian dari Turkmenistan. Beliau tumbuh dewasa dalam salah satu abad paling subur secara intelektual dalam peradaban Islam, ketika kota-kota besar Khurasan, Baghdad, dan Nishapur melahirkan ulama-ulama terkemuka di berbagai disiplin ilmu — teologi, filsafat, fikih, hadis, tata bahasa, dan puisi. Al-Zamakhshari menguasai beberapa disiplin ini namun meraih ketenaran abadi dalam dua bidang: linguistik Arab dan tafsir Al-Quran.

Beliau sangat mencintai bahasa Arab sebagai bahasa dan Al-Quran sebagai ekspresi sastra dan ilahi tertinggi. Beliau melakukan beberapa kali perjalanan ke Mekah, pusat spiritual dunia Islam, di mana beliau tinggal untuk periode yang panjang dan mendapat gelar kehormatan 'Jar Allah' — Tetangga Allah — yang mencerminkan kedekatan fisiknya dengan Masjidil Haram sekaligus penghormatan komunitas keilmuan terhadapnya. Dikisahkan bahwa beliau berjalan kaki meski menderita cacat fisik yang menyulitkannya, dan dedikasinya terhadap keilmuan di tengah keterbatasan itu menjadi bagian dari statusnya yang legendaris.

Al-Zamakhshari adalah penulis yang sangat produktif. Karya-karyanya mencakup tata bahasa Arab (al-Mufassal, pembahasan komprehensif tentang morfologi dan sintaksis Arab yang menjadi teks referensi utama), leksikografi Arab (al-Fa'iq fi Gharib al-Hadith, tentang kosakata langka dalam literatur hadis), adab dan peribahasa, serta teologi. Namun komentar Qurannya, Al-Kashaf, yang mengukuhkan tempatnya secara permanen dalam sejarah keilmuan Islam — dipuji oleh para pengagumnya karena kejeniusan linguistiknya dan dikaji secara kritis oleh para ulama yang tidak sependapat dengan premis teologisnya.

Pembentukan intelektualnya berlangsung dalam mazhab teologi Mu'tazilah — mazhab rasionalis yang berkembang khususnya di Basra dan Baghdad dari abad kedua hingga kelima era Islam, yang dikenal dengan doktrin-doktrinnya tentang kemakhlukan Al-Quran, keadilan Ilahi, dan peran esensial akal dalam teologi. Pada zaman al-Zamakhshari, Mu'tazilisme sebagian besar telah tergeser oleh teologi Asy'ari dan Maturidi sebagai arus utama ortodoksi Sunni, namun tetap bertahan sebagai tradisi intelektual yang hidup di sebagian Asia Tengah dan di kalangan ulama berorientasi rasionalis.

Komitmen Mu'tazili al-Zamakhshari tidak dapat dipisahkan dari tafsirnya. Cara beliau membaca ayat-ayat tertentu, komentar teologis yang disisipkannya, dan alternatif-alternatif yang ditolaknya semuanya dibentuk oleh doktrin Mu'tazilah. Inilah mengapa Al-Kashaf sekaligus menjadi salah satu teks yang paling dihargai dan paling dibaca secara cermat dalam tradisi tafsir: wawasan linguistiknya tak tertandingi, namun intervensi teologisnya harus dikenali dan dievaluasi berhadapan dengan konsensus keilmuan Sunni.
content_ar:
Abu al-Qasim Mahmud ibn Umar al-Zamakhshari (467–538 AH / 1075–1144 CE) was born in the town of Zamakhshar in the Khwarazm region of Central Asia, in what is today Turkmenistan. He came of age in one of the most intellectually fertile centuries of Islamic civilization, when the great cities of Khurasan, Baghdad, and Nishapur were producing scholars of the highest caliber across every discipline — theology, philosophy, jurisprudence, hadith, grammar, and poetry. Al-Zamakhshari mastered several of these disciplines but achieved his most enduring fame in two: Arabic linguistics and Quranic exegesis.

He was deeply devoted to Arabic as a language and to the Quran as its supreme literary and divine expression. He made multiple journeys to Mecca, the spiritual center of the Islamic world, where he resided for extended periods and earned the honorific title 'Jar الله' — Neighbor of God — reflecting both his physical proximity to the Haram and the reverence in which the scholarly community held him. He reportedly walked despite a physical disability that caused him considerable hardship, and his dedication to scholarship in the face of this limitation became part of his legendary status.

Al-Zamakhshari was a prolific author. His works span Arabic grammar (the Mufassal, a comprehensive treatment of Arabic morphology and syntax that became a major reference text), Arabic lexicography (al-Fa'iq fi Gharib al-Hadith, on rare vocabulary in hadith literature), adab and proverbs, and theology. But it is his Quranic commentary, Al-Kashaf, that earned him a permanent place in the history of Islamic scholarship — praised by admirers for its linguistic genius and studied critically by scholars who disagree with its theological premises.

His intellectual formation took place within the Mu'tazilite theological school — the rationalist school that flourished particularly in Basra and Baghdad from the second through fifth centuries of the Islamic era, known for its doctrines on the createdness of the Quran, divine justice, and the essential role of reason in theology. By al-Zamakhshari's time, Mu'tazilism had largely been displaced by Ash'ari and Maturidi theology as the mainstream of Sunni orthodoxy, but it continued as a living intellectual tradition in parts of Central Asia and among scholars with rationalist orientations.

Al-Zamakhshari's Mu'tazili commitments are inseparable from his tafsir. The way he reads certain passages, the theological glosses he inserts, and the alternatives he rejects are all shaped by Mu'tazili doctrine. This is why Al-Kashaf is simultaneously one of the most prized and most carefully read texts in the tafsir tradition: its linguistic insights are unparalleled, but its theological interventions must be recognized and evaluated against the Sunni scholarly consensus.
